meta.purpose: Mission Control: data brief for Mission North East (Sunderland, South Tyneside) and Mission Coastal (Scarborough, Hastings). Use ONLY these figures; never invent numbers.
meta.vintages: KS4 attainment 2024/25 (LAD workbooks); KS2 2024/25 revised; absence 2024/25; 16-17 NEET 2025 (Dec 2024-Feb 2025 avg); KS4 destinations 2022/23 cohort; IDACI 2025.
meta.definitions: A8 = Attainment 8. Disadv = disadvantaged (FSM ever-6). RWM = KS2 reading/writing/maths expected standard. PA = persistent absence (10%+ sessions missed), secondary unless stated. NEET-or-not-known combines confirmed 16-17 NEET with activity-not-known (a tracking gap). "No sustained destination" (ns) is the school-level NEET proxy from KS4 destinations 2022/23.

sunderland.name: Sunderland
sunderland.summary: Phase 1 Mission NE cluster. Sustained below-average KS4 attainment, among the highest disadvantaged underperformance in the region, elevated persistent absence, high deprivation (53rd most deprived nationally on IDACI), and no existing targeted DfE support (RISE) — genuine additionality.
sunderland.composite_rank_ne: 2nd weakest of 12 NE LAs on the combined ranking (score 53)

south-tyneside.name: South Tyneside
south-tyneside.summary: Phase 1 Mission NE cluster. A8 of 42.7 vs NE average 44.1 and national 46.0; disadvantaged A8 33.1, nearly two points below the national average; elevated persistent absence; 48th most deprived nationally on IDACI; not covered by RISE or other targeted programmes.
south-tyneside.composite_rank_ne: Joint 2nd weakest of 12 NE LAs on the combined ranking (score 53)

strongest_rwm.name: St Bede's Catholic Primary School, Jarrow

scarborough.name: Scarborough
scarborough.summary: Mission Coastal (north). Chosen by Ministers from a data-led shortlist of coastal LADs. Disadvantaged A8 of 26.6 is among the weakest nationally. KS2 is notably weak: over half of primaries below national average, so the mission includes a strong primary attainment focus. Strong trusts (e.g. Delta) have recently taken on weak secondaries but pupil engagement remains the core challenge. Former Opportunity Area and Priority Education Investment Area.
scarborough.idaciNote: LAD-level IDACI not published on the same basis; district contains some of the most deprived coastal neighbourhoods in Yorkshire.
scarborough.neetNote: 16-17 NEET is only published at LA level: figures shown are North Yorkshire, which will understate the Scarborough picture.
scarborough.programmes: Opportunity Area (North Yorkshire coast), PEIA 2023-25, RISE at George Pindar and Graham schools.

secondary_schools.name: St Augustine's Catholic School - a Catholic voluntary academy
secondary_schools.trust: ST CUTHBERT'S ROMAN CATHOLIC ACADEMY TRUST

strongest_rwm.name: St Martin's Church of England Voluntary Aided Primary School, Scarborough
Lythe Church of England Voluntary Controlled Primary School

hastings.name: Hastings
hastings.summary: Mission Coastal (south). Disadvantaged A8 of 26.0 — the lowest of any coastal area in England for three years running. Defining challenge is churn: high pupil mobility driven by housing insecurity, plus a very large elective home education cohort (~10% of secondary-age pupils). Former Opportunity Area and PEIA.
hastings.neetNote: 16-17 NEET is only published at LA level: figures shown are East Sussex, which will understate the Hastings picture.
hastings.programmes: Opportunity Area, PEIA, RISE at 1 secondary.

weakest_rwm.name: Christ Church CofE Primary and Nursery Academy
Sacred Heart Catholic Primary School, Hastings

strongest_rwm.name: Guestling Bradshaw Church of England Primary School

strands.name: Intensive School Improvement
strands.text: Performance Support Teams of experienced leaders and specialists from high-performing trusts deployed into clusters of 6-8 secondary schools, working alongside local staff on attendance, behaviour, reading and KS3 catch-up. Practitioner-led, not consultancy.

strands.name: Pupil Engagement & Enrichment
strands.text: A structured enrichment and mentoring offer for every child in the mission areas, coordinated with the national Enrichment Framework and the DCMS £22.5m investment: sport, arts, careers, mentoring, work experience, delivered with anchor partners Right to Succeed, AllChild and Impetus.

strands.name: Test, Learn & Grow
strands.text: Place-based TLG projects, micro-RCTs and multi-agency pilots, scoped bottom-up with local partners and supported by a Cabinet Office-funded satellite team. The missions as the R&D arm of the English school system.

strands.name: Leadership Capacity
strands.text: Building the capacity and development of school leaders: place-led, focused on disadvantaged communities, with a long-term pipeline (Leadership Programmes North East plus philanthropic match).

lines.note: ~25 specialists @ £800/day, ~120 days each, clusters of 8-12 schools
One experienced educationalist per mission on secondment
PM, two programme officers, admin, board secretariat
£250k per mission: backfill, TLG project leads, innovation pots
Top-up to Pupil Engagement Framework and Enrichment Framework rollout
Real-time dashboard, impact evaluation, research partnership
Leadership Programmes North East + philanthropic match

tlg_concepts.name: National Rapid Evidence Network
tlg_concepts.text: Micro-trial infrastructure in mission schools growing into an opt-in rapid evidence network any school in England can join.

tlg_concepts.name: Child Engagement Early Warning System
tlg_concepts.text: Join DfE attendance with NHS, social care and youth service signals into a single safeguarding-grade view per child, with predictive flags tested at the Y6-7 transition and a tested human response paired to every flag.

tlg_concepts.name: Family Engagement Guarantee
tlg_concepts.text: Trials of structured parental engagement: text-nudges, community parent mentors, family learning, reverse parents’ evenings — targeting white working-class families reporting the lowest trust in schools.

tlg_concepts.name: Enrichment Passport & outcome-linked commissioning
tlg_concepts.text: A digital enrichment passport for every mission pupil; providers paid partly on verified participation of disadvantaged pupils.

tlg_concepts.name: The Year 7 Bridge
tlg_concepts.text: The Y6-7 transition as a designed experience: summer bridge on secondary sites, one childhood one record, KS3 engagement curricula, transition mentors from Y9-10.

tlg_concepts.name: Inclusion Labs
tlg_concepts.text: Volunteer mission schools test inclusive mainstream models the SEND reform programme needs evidence for.

tlg_concepts.name: KS3 Micro-Trial Partnership
tlg_concepts.text: Deepen the WhatWorked Education partnership into the country’s first secondary classroom-practice trial unit embedded in PST cluster schools; every PST intervention becomes a trial arm.

tlg_concepts.name: “Made in the North East” industry pathways
tlg_concepts.text: Employer-embedded enrichment at scale using advanced manufacturing, electrification, offshore energy and Crown Works Studios: workplace encounters from Y7, employer-sponsored KS3 projects, technical tasters.

tlg_concepts.name: “Every Mover Known”
tlg_concepts.text: Rapid-integration protocol for mid-year movers (48-hour records transfer, automatic enrichment and mentoring enrolment, named integration lead) plus a coastal workforce premium trial.

tlg_concepts.name: “The Year-Round Coast”
tlg_concepts.text: Year-round opportunity model for a seasonal economy: holiday enrichment, paid summer roles with tourism/offshore wind/marine sectors, a coastal curriculum, school-year scheduling flexibilities.

vmost_current_frame.vision: Every child achieving and thriving — a country where the place a child grows up, from the North East to the coast, no longer sets a ceiling on how far they go.
vmost_current_frame.mission: Place-based missions in the North East and coastal towns: a whole-government commitment to children, inside the school gates and beyond them — practitioner-led, starting where need is greatest. Focus areas: South Tyneside, Sunderland, Hastings, Scarborough.

objectives.title: Narrow the gap
objectives.detail: Narrow the 17.7-point White British FSM attainment gap against pre-mission baselines.

strategies.title: Efficacy engine
strategies.detail: Teacher × pupil × parental efficacy connected by culture, relationships and accountability.

tactics.title: PSTs deployed to clusters of 6-8 secondaries
tactics.detail: Specialists from high-performing trusts working on attendance, behaviour, reading, KS3 catch-up.

objectives.title: Raise attainment
objectives.detail: Lift Attainment 8 towards the England average (46.0). NE 44.1; Hastings disadvantaged 26.0.

strategies.title: One integrated model
strategies.detail: Four strands delivered together, centred on place.

tactics.title: Shared reading-fluency app + KS3 early intervention
tactics.detail: Across the cohort in cluster schools.

objectives.title: Cut absence
objectives.detail: Reduce overall and persistent absence, tracked termly.

strategies.title: Engagement beyond the gate
strategies.detail: Attendance is a symptom; engagement of pupil, parent and community is the driver.

tactics.title: Daily attendance monitoring + family liaison
tactics.detail: Persistent-absence protocols in every cluster school.

objectives.title: Enrich
objectives.detail: Guaranteed enrichment participation for every pupil as a mission metric.

strategies.title: Collective impact partnership
strategies.detail: Anchor partners Right to Succeed, AllChild, Impetus + DCMS £22.5m.

tactics.title: Enrichment entitlement aligned with DCMS investment
tactics.detail: Sport, arts, careers, mentoring, work experience.

strategies.title: Phased scaling
strategies.detail: Plan summer 2026, deliver from Sept 2026, a new cluster each term: refine before we scale.

patterns.title: Deprivation does not explain MCS performance
patterns.text: Across the rest of North Yorkshire disadvantage strongly predicts results (correlation -0.43). Within MCS it is essentially zero (-0.05). Some of the most deprived schools are the best performers (Airy Hill 45% disadvantaged, 85% RWM; Thomas Hinderwell 50%, 77%). The problem is school-level, not demographic — fixable, with local proof points.

patterns.title: The girls’ advantage has vanished
patterns.text: Elsewhere in the county girls outperform boys by 6-7pp on RWM. In MCS schools girls achieve 58% vs 66% for girls elsewhere (a 7.7pp gap, vs 2.9pp for boys). At Lindhead only 7% of girls reached the expected standard against 35% of boys.

patterns.title: Reading is strong; maths blocks the combined measure
patterns.text: Several schools post strong reading but weak RWM (Fylingdales reads at 93% yet only 40% achieve RWM). Maths is the binding constraint in most cases, writing in a few.

hastings_out_of_provision.note: Hastings postcode area, Years 7-11, mid-2026. Secondary-age children out of, or at risk of being out of, provision.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Improve tracking and ownership (no young person unknown)
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: About 314k 16-24s are out of sight; cannot re-engage who you cannot see.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Tackle persistent absence
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Persistent absence KS4 is 2nd-strongest NEET risk factor (+10ppt).

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Mandatory Year 7 risk screening + mentoring
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Targets EHC plan (+16-20ppt) and absence; near-zero data cost.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Work-experience entitlement (4+ employer contacts)
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: 4+ employer contacts: 5x less likely NEET; ~£60 placement for ~£150 benefit.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Tilt apprenticeships to young people
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Best youth intervention (10 in work per 100); ~£15 back per £1 (L2, 19-23).

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Reform post-16 maths/English resit
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Only ~a third improve on resit; repeated failure is a NEET pathway.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: High-quality vocational pathways at KS4
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Tech awards: 23% lower unauthorised absence; absence drives NEET.

policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.name: Open up admissions to the best schools
policy_levers_with_modelled_impact.evidence: Selective schools 0.85% no sustained destination vs 5.54% non-selective.

levers_note: Modelled maximum reduction in the national 16-24 NEET count at full rollout, with indicative national annual cost (£m). Ready to Work sprint modelling, July 2026. Mission-area pilots would be a small fraction of the national cost.

key_stats.label: lower unauthorised absence for pupils who take at least one tech award, yet 16% of schools do not offer them and 59% of employers do not understand them
less likely to be NEET for pupils who recall four or more employer contacts at school, controlling for qualifications and background
cost versus net benefit per work placement: good value, but it is repeated employer contact that lowers NEET risk
sustained destination one year after KS4 for alternative provision versus mainstream pupils
the 16-19 funding formula uses lagged data and does not fully reimburse in-year growth, with no ringfenced NEET re-engagement funding
spend per student in 16-18 colleges versus school sixth forms; yet the median FE teacher earns £10,400 less than a secondary teacher
the 16-17 employment rate has more than halved since the 1990s
under-19 apprenticeship starts are near a historic low, about 40% below their early-2010s peak
sustained destination rate for the 16-18 Level 3 cohort from FE colleges, the lowest of any setting, against 88% for mainstream schools
of 18-year-olds are NEET; 34% are in higher education and 20% in work without education or training
extra young people in work for every 100 supported through apprenticeships, the most effective youth employment intervention (wage subsidies: 3)
return on a Level 2 apprenticeship for 19-23 year olds; youth apprenticeships cost about £11k each
more likely to be continuously employed at 25 for Level 3 apprenticeship completers than similar peers with a Level 2 qualification
of women and men respectively are financially worse off for going to university, with returns varying hugely by subject

Secondary education: Develop high-quality vocational and applied pathways at Key Stage 4
Ensure every young person leaves with a clear destination and is prepared for the transition
Widen access to the highest-performing schools
Reprioritise Key Stage 3 and strengthen Key Stage 4 curriculum delivery
Improve careers education and make learning feel more relevant
Tertiary education: Massively expand apprenticeship opportunities in white working class communities
Empower FE colleges as high-quality local engines of progression
Reduce the financial risks of further and higher study; free local transport for under-21s
Early & primary: Build a stronger network of Best Start Family Hubs
Extend 30 hours' free childcare to all disadvantaged families
Make reading fluency a national priority
Across the system: Define and prioritise white working class outcomes
A more inclusive approach to SEND and additional needs
A stronger response to mental health and excessive screen use

outcome.y3: 75% move out of risk (matching AllChild benchmark)

outcome.y1: Narrow gap to national by 0.5 pts
outcome.y3: Narrow gap to national by 2.0 pts

strand2_partners.role: Backbone organisation and collective impact coordinator
strand2_partners.ne: Primary backbone for South Tyneside & Sunderland. Leads community engagement, shared measurement, partner convening.??
strand2_partners.coastal: Advisory role, supporting backbone methodology transfer to Scarborough.

strand2_partners.role: Link Worker deployment, Impact Programme delivery, outcomes-based funding model
strand2_partners.ne: Link Worker deployment in mission schools. Training and QA of pastoral support model.
strand2_partners.coastal: Primary backbone for Hastings (building on £3m programme launching September 2026). Link Workers in six schools expanding to full coverage.

strand2_partners.role: Capacity builder, impact funder, evidence partner
strand2_partners.ne: Identifying and strengthening best local delivery organisations. Funding due diligence on enrichment providers. Policy insight and coalition building.
strand2_partners.coastal: Same capacity-building role. Connect Fund and Engage Fund alignment for mission organisations.

strand2_partners.role: Strategic oversight, policy alignment, core funding
strand2_partners.ne: £4.2m annual allocation (shared across all four strands). Enrichment Framework and Pupil Engagement Framework alignment.
strand2_partners.coastal: Same. DCMS £22.5m enrichment funding coordination. Cabinet Office TLG team alignment.

strand2_partners.role: Statutory duties, local intelligence, service integration
strand2_partners.ne: South Tyneside and Sunderland Councils: attendance, social care, early years, housing, SEND.
strand2_partners.coastal: East Sussex CC (Hastings) and North Yorkshire Council (Scarborough): same statutory integration.

strand2_risks.impact: Schools and LAs already face multiple demands; collective working adds coordination burden.
strand2_risks.mitigation: Backbone organisations absorb coordination cost. Streamlined governance with clear decision rights. ‘No meeting without purpose’ principle.

strand2_risks.impact: Insufficient local organisations to deliver the enrichment entitlement.
strand2_risks.mitigation: Impetus capacity-building role: identify, fund and strengthen local providers. National partners (Sport England, Arts Council) deploy mobile/outreach provision. Digital enrichment as supplement.

strand2_risks.impact: Difficulty recruiting skilled Link Workers in deprived coastal/northern areas.
strand2_risks.mitigation: Competitive pay scales benchmarked to local market. AllChild training and career development pathway. University placement partnerships.

strand2_risks.impact: GDPR and institutional caution inhibit the shared measurement system.
strand2_risks.mitigation: Data sharing agreements established in Phase 1 as condition of Charter. DfE legal team support. Pseudonymised data where pupil-level sharing is not possible.

strand2_risks.impact: Change of Ministers or Spending Review decisions could defund the missions.
strand2_risks.mitigation: Build cross-party support through early evidence publication. Sustainability plan from Year 2. Philanthropic match funding provides buffer.

enrichment_benchmarks.benchmark: 1. Developing a stable and integrated enrichment programme
enrichment_benchmarks.summary: Every school should have an embedded programme of enrichment activities that is known and understood by pupils, parents and carers, staff, governors, external providers and the wider local community. ​

enrichment_benchmarks.benchmark: 2. Sustaining a broad and balanced enrichment programme​
enrichment_benchmarks.summary: Every pupil should have multiple and varied opportunities to engage in purposeful enrichment activities that are fun and can support a thriving childhood and successful transition to adulthood, including through supporting wellbeing and social skills.
Every school should align its enrichment programme with its broader priorities, for instance around attainment, attendance, behaviour, careers, curriculum, personal development and wellbeing.

enrichment_benchmarks.benchmark: 4. Responding to the interests, passions and leadership potential of every pupil, and of staff​
enrichment_benchmarks.summary: The enrichment programme should be created with involvement from the whole school community and the wider locality, with a particular focus on pupils’ voice and choice.​

enrichment_benchmarks.benchmark: 5. Targeting and meeting the needs of pupils at risk of missing out​
enrichment_benchmarks.summary: Opportunities and access points should be tailored to the needs of every pupil, including any additional needs of vulnerable and disadvantaged pupils, young people with SEND, young carers and those who are absent.​
Every child and young person should have opportunities to experience enrichment activities led by high-quality external providers, in addition to opportunities led within the school.​

enrichment_benchmarks.benchmark: 7. Agreeing and sharing outcomes ​OR Impact and recognition
enrichment_benchmarks.summary: Every school should reflect on the key pupil-level outcomes their enrichment programme aims to develop and acknowledge and celebrate participation and achievement in enrichment.
Every school should put consistent mechanisms in place to understand and improve the quality and impact of any enrichment activity they provide. ​

policy_context.whitePaper: Every Child Achieving and Thriving (Feb 2026): ten-year plan; targets national average A8 of 50 by end of decade and halving the disadvantage gap within a generation. Three shifts: narrow→broad (knowledge-rich curriculum + guaranteed enrichment), plus inclusion/SEND (£3.7bn specialist capital, disadvantage funding moving beyond FSM) and phased delivery 2025-28 (align to best practice → prepare for reform → deliver). Enrichment Framework + Ofsted benchmarks from autumn; new KS3 alliance for primary-secondary transition.
policy_context.inclusionFramework: National Inclusion Framework (internal direction paper): minimum National Inclusion Standards for every school by 2028, £15m evidence base via independent expert panel. Framed as a floor not a ceiling; key design risk is compliance-driven floor standards. Mission Inclusion Labs generate the delivery evidence this framework needs.
policy_context.enrichmentBenchmarksNote: Eight School Enrichment Benchmarks (Sept 2025) — the national framework the mission enrichment entitlement aligns to.
